attributes.stem:
A 22-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a rash around her mouth. She noticed raised bumps yesterday afternoon that are slightly itchy and sore to touch. She has not experienced similar lesions before. She has no significant medical history and takes the combined oral contraceptive pill regularly.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There is a cluster of raised bumps from the left corner of her lip to the chin, with some showing a small amount of serous fluid oozing from where they have opened. There are no lesions inside the mouth or on the lips. A small, tender lymph node is palpable in the submental region, which she was unaware of.

She is scheduled to speak at a local community event in two days and is anxious to know if there are treatment options to reduce the appearance of the rash.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate treatment?
options: Advise that no treatments necessary and it will self-resolve
attributes.explanation:
This clinical presentation of clustered papules and vesicles with serous fluid is highly suggestive of primary herpes simplex virus (HSV) infection. A swab should be collected to confirm the diagnosis, but this should not delay treatment. Lymphadenopathy and flu-like symptoms may be present in a primary infection.

Oral famciclovir is the most appropriate treatment in this case. Whilst most episodes of HSV will self-resolve, this patient has requested treatment. A single dose of 1500 mg is effective against HSV and can help reduce the duration and severity of symptoms when given early in the course of the infection. Treatment is particularly important for this patient given her upcoming speaking engagement. Topical aciclovir 5% is an alternative option, requiring application five times a day. For intraoral lesions, famciclovir is recommended, while for cutaneous lesions, either famciclovir or aciclovir can be used.

Advising to keep the area clean and allow self-resolution is not the most appropriate management for this patient. While HSV lesions will eventually self-resolve, antiviral treatment can significantly reduce the duration of symptoms and viral shedding, which is particularly important for this patient.

Oral doxycycline is an antibiotic often used to treat acne, perioral dermatitis, or rosacea. While these conditions can cause facial lesions, they typically present with papules and pustules rather than vesicles and have more pronounced erythema than is seen in HSV. Presentation is less acute, with a more symmetrical distribution and perioral dermatitis often involves the nasolabial folds.

Angular cheilitis presents with painful fissures, blisters and redness around the corner of the mouth. There are multiple underlying causes, including irritant dermatitis, nutritional deficiency and fungal infection. Topical clotrimazole is an appropriate treatment if the cause is thought to be a fungal infection, however this presentation is not consistent with angular cheilitis. Typically, inflammation is localised to the corner of the mouth and does not extend to the chin and lymphadenopathy would not be present.

Topical hydrocortisone is a mild corticosteroid that may be used to treat atopic dermatitis. While some forms of eczema can present with pustules and vesicles, this type of presentation is limited to the hands and feet, not the face. Hydrocortisone would not be appropriate for a suspected HSV infection as it may potentially worsen the viral infection by suppressing the local immune response.

It's important to note that while HSV is the most likely diagnosis based on the clinical presentation, other conditions such as acne, rosacea, impetigo, and molluscum contagiosum should be considered in the differential diagnosis. The acute onset, painful nature, and vesicular appearance of the lesions strongly support HSV as the most likely cause. Prompt treatment with oral antivirals can significantly improve symptoms.
learningPoints: Primary herpes simplex virus infection typically presents with clustered vesicles and papules on an erythematous base.
Oral famciclovir 1500 mg as a single dose is an effective treatment for primary herpes simplex virus infection.
references: Cernik C, Gallina K, Brodell RT. TThe Treatment of Herpes Simplex InfectionsAn Evidence-Based Review. Archives of Internal Medicine. 2008 Jun 9;168(11):1137. Available at: https://jamanetwork.com/journals/jamainternalmedicine/fullarticle/414294

Piperi E, Papadopoulou E, Georgaki M, Dovrat S, Bar Illan M, Nikitakis NG, et al. Management of oral herpes simplex virus infections: The problem of resistance. A narrative review. Oral Diseases [Internet]. 2023 Jun 6; Available at: https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/full/10.1111/odi.14635
